# What’s Happening in K-pop Right Now? A Shift You Can’t Ignore 2026

## Explore the biggest shifts, comebacks, and global moves that defined the K-pop industry in Q1 2026.

## Over the past year, multiple reports have pointed to a noticeable slowdown in domestic album sales within the K-pop industry.

This reflects shifting consumer behavior, as well as signs of market saturation within South Korea’s core fanbase. This pressure has prompted entertainment companies and artists to rapidly recalibrate their strategies, with a stronger and more deliberate pivot toward the global market.

From expanding international tours and collaborating with global artists to prioritizing platforms with worldwide reach, the industry is clearly restructuring its approach.

Embed from @[kchartmaster](https://x.com/kchartsmaster/status/1874082432867328013?s=20)

Now that the first quarter (Q1) of 2026 has concluded, the conversation is no longer centered solely on where K-pop is headed, but on whether these global expansion strategies are already producing tangible results.

This article explores what unfolded during the first quarter of 2026, examining how the industry has responded to these ongoing shifts and what they may signal for the future of K-pop.

## The End of the Peak Album Sales Era

This does not suggest that album sales are no longer important; rather, it reflects a strategic shift toward reducing reliance on them as a primary source of revenue.

For years, physical album sales have been heavily driven by fandom behaviors such as bulk buying and collectible culture. This raises an important question about what truly defines success: do high sales still reflect genuine popularity, or simply the purchasing power of dedicated fans?

While album sales remain one of the most tangible indicators of success, it is important to recognize that fans’ spending capacity is ultimately limited. Even for those who wish to support their favorite artists on charts, financial constraints can make this difficult, and it is unrealistic to expect individuals to purchase large quantities of the same album purely for collection purposes.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Image 4: Kpop industry 2026](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*b2k5wDJiTrA_CSSeqwC97A.png)

Photo from canva

More importantly, album sales tend to provide only a short-term boost, with their impact largely concentrated in the early stages of a release cycle. They can drive strong initial chart performance, but are less indicative of sustained, long-term engagement.

As a result, entertainment companies are gradually shifting their focus toward more sustainable and globally recognized metrics, such as streaming numbers, touring revenue, and brand partnerships. This transition signals a deeper structural shift in how success within the K-pop industry is defined.

## Scaling Beyond the Peninsula

With slowing growth in local album sales and audience saturation, agencies are increasingly designing strategies with overseas markets in mind first. South Korea remains culturally important, but economically, it is becoming just one of many markets rather than the core driver of growth.

Clear examples of this shift can be seen in the release of English-language singles, scheduling releases on Fridays to align with the U.S. chart tracking system, prioritizing international tours over promotions on Korean music shows, and establishing business operations overseas.

Major agencies are no longer simply exporting K-pop from Seoul — they are building infrastructure directly within global markets.

One of the clearest examples is **HYBE,** which has aggressively expanded its overseas operations by establishing regional headquarters and subsidiaries around the world. Following the launches of **HYBE America, HYBE Japan, HYBE Latin America, and HYBE China,** the company recently established a local subsidiary in India, marking its fifth overseas headquarters.

This global network reflects how K-pop companies are evolving into multinational entertainment corporations, positioning themselves closer to local audiences through artist management, label partnerships, touring, and talent development strategies tailored to each region.

![Image 5: Kpop industry 2026](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/0*hiMZlJy9c3a5zocx.png)

Photo from [HYBE](https://hybecorp.com/eng/company/info)

## The Comebacks of Groups with Massive Fandoms

The anticipated comebacks of**BTS** and **BLACKPINK** in Q1 2026 are expected to significantly boost South Korea’s album export growth compared to the previous year. The scale of their global fanbases enables immediate, large-scale consumption upon release, particularly in international markets.

The Hankook Ilbo, the sister publication of The Korea Times, reported that BLACKPINK’s latest album **‘_Deadline’_** recorded 1.46 million copies in first-day sales, setting a new record for the highest first-day sales by a K-pop girl group.

In addition, the group’s comeback drove YG Entertainment’s stock on the KOSDAQ up by 6,100 won (8.56%) as of 10.30 a.m. on February 27th, 2026 compared to the previous trading day.

Original information from @[circlechart](https://www.instagram.com/p/DW5v0MfAbWf/?utm_source=ig_web_copy_link&igsh=NTc4MTIwNjQ2YQ%3D%3D)

According to HYBE, the agency behind BTS, its revenue for January to March increased by 39.5% year-on-year to 468 million USD. First-day album sales reached approximately 3.98 million copies, marking a record high for the first quarter, which is typically considered a slow season for the music industry.

It is also projected that BTS will generate substantial additional revenue following the conclusion of the **_BTS ‘ARIRANG’ World Tour_**, one of the largest concert tours in K-pop history.

Moreover, global acts like BTS generate a broader economic impact, with estimates suggesting they contribute billions of dollars annually to South Korea’s economy.

At the same time, the worldwide popularity of both groups strengthens the country’s soft power, further increasing demand for Korean products and culture. Therefore, their comebacks are not merely musical events but key drivers of South Korea’s cultural exports and overall economic growth on a global scale.

Embed from @[allkpop](https://x.com/allkpop/status/2035198081034047515?s=20)

## “Fanomenon” The K-Coachella Project by the Big 4

The continued presence of K-pop acts at major global festivals reflects how the industry has secured a place on international stages. At the same time, increased alignment and indirect collaboration among the Big 4 companies signal a shared recognition that global expansion requires scale, coordination, and industry-wide positioning rather than isolated competition.

Embed from @[billboardkorea](https://x.com/billboardkorea/status/2044707434204467317?s=20)

**_“Fanomenon”_** is a global-scale Korean music festival project established through a joint venture between South Korea’s four major entertainment powerhouses: HYBE, SM Entertainment, YG Entertainment, and JYP Entertainment.

The initiative is led by Park Jin-young, Chairman of the Popular Culture Exchange Committee. The name “_Fanomenon”_ combines the words “fan” and “phenomenon,” emphasizing the global power of fandom culture.

Beyond its massive scale, _Fanomenon_ serves as a strategic experiment to explore innovative methods for distributing Korean culture on the global stage. The project is scheduled to debut in South Korea in December 2027, with plans to evolve into a global touring festival reaching major cities worldwide starting in May 2028.

Embed from @[aju_press](https://x.com/aju_press/status/2044989468130885659?s=20)

## Expansion into New Areas

The K-pop industry has transitioned from a mainstream global trend to a more strategic model of deep market penetration, targeting localized audiences and establishing cultural roots worldwide.

This shift has not happened overnight, but rather builds on earlier milestones. Previously, **ATEEZ** made history as the first K-pop act to perform on the main stage of the Mawazine Festival in Morocco, drawing over 200,000 attendees and opening doors to the North African market.

Building on this trajectory, similar patterns of localized cultural integration have continued to emerge in other regions. In Brazil, **NMIXX** successfully embedded themselves into one of the country’s most iconic traditions by performing at the Brazilian Carnival, attracting a massive crowd of over 3 million people at the Bloco da Pabllo.

As special guests of the legendary Brazilian artist Pabllo Vittar, their appearance marked a rare instance of K-pop directly participating in a deeply rooted local celebration.

The performance received widespread praise for their live vocals, further demonstrating K-pop’s ability to not only enter but actively thrive within the core of regional cultural events.

Embed from [@billboard](https://www.tiktok.com/@billboard/video/7607905335327657229?q=nmixx+carnival+brazil&t=1778161556614)

Furthermore, **BTS** elevated the genre’s status to a diplomatic level through a formal meeting with the President of Mexico at the National Palace, an event that drew 50,000 fans to the Zócalo square.

Collectively, these milestones prove that K-pop has transcended geographical and linguistic barriers, becoming a powerful and permanent fixture within global cultural and political institutions.

Embed from @[Claudiashein](https://x.com/Claudiashein/status/2052167260299465198?s=20)

Embed from @[cnn](https://www.tiktok.com/@cnn/video/7637016373956300045?is_from_webapp=1&sender_device=pc&web_id=7606390871018702344)

These acts signify a major evolution of K-pop, elevating it from a global entertainment trend to a formidable “National Soft Power” that permeates both the cultural roots and political symbols of the West.

Beyond traditional concert tours, K-pop is now asserting its influence through cultural diplomacy and massive public engagement.

The ability to mobilize millions and command such significant diplomatic attention proves that K-pop has become a vital bridge, connecting global economies and cultural identities through the power of borderless communication.

## Nostalgia Strategy

The comeback of legendary groups such as BIGBANG, EXO, and Super Junior represents a strategic masterclass in merging “nostalgia with global scale.”

By leveraging a deeply loyal fanbase with significant purchasing power, entertainment agencies effectively minimize financial risks compared to the costs of launching new acts.

Furthermore, elevating these artists onto major international stages serves to rebrand them — transitioning them from traditional idols into timeless Global Icons.

This strategy not only preserves their legacy but also ensures their continued relevance in an ever-evolving global market.

Embed from @[TheePopCore](https://x.com/TheePopCore/status/2043592145093947571?s=20)

This not only introduces pioneering K-Pop to a new generation of Western audiences but also establishes a brand heritage for the agencies, proving their ability to sustain long-term artistry.

Ultimately, these reunions create a powerful halo effect that benefits junior artists while reinforcing the idea that high-quality, legendary music remains relevant and influential in the global market, regardless of the era.

Embed from @[PCY_BASE](https://x.com/PCY_BASE/status/2051959595673739462?s=20)

## In Summary

However, this growth comes with a new set of challenges. Competition in the global market is intensifying, marketing costs are rising, and structural questions remain about how much of its core identity K-pop can retain amid its push toward globalization.

Taken together, these trends suggest that this is not just another big year for K-pop, but a critical turning point. The industry is being tested on its ability to evolve beyond a fandom-driven model into a globally sustainable ecosystem.

The real question is no longer whether K-pop can expand internationally, but whether it can maintain long-term relevance in an increasingly competitive global market.

title: Results of Kpopalypse’s predictions for 2025 and all new mega trufaxual predictions for 2026 – KPOPALYPSE
content:
Rock gradually goes out of style again – 2025 mainly just taught k-pop agencies that none of them know how to actually write a decent rock song, so during 2026 they’ll gradually wind down their attempts at milking a culture they don’t understand and have their artists go back to sitting politely in wooden chairs in front of flower arrangements or whatever it is that people who are out of their depth making rock music think a good use of their artists’ time is.

Boy groups improve – with BTS’s legacy waning, there’s room for a new competitior to step up to the plate. Don’t expect the same level of commercial success with these ventures, but we might just get a banger or two along the way. [...] LGBT is here to stay – now that the initial doors have been knocked down and nobody’s career died from the impact, you can expect regular coming-outs from k-pop alumni as just part of the normal k-pop landscape now.

AI song controversies ahoy – if you think the use of AI in k-pop concept art and music videos is a concern, just wait until an agency gets caught using AI to write their entire artist catalog. [...] Idols get more risque on social media – CORRECT. One of the big shifts caused by the influence of “global girl groups” with more relaxed skin exposure rules, plus Blackpink’s more recent live stages designed with global rather than Korean audiences in mind, is that the needle for what’s acceptable k-pop fashion is moving ever-so-slightly back in the 2015 direction. Watch out, there could be more Kpopalypse fashion class posts coming in 2026!

# Results of Kpopalypse’s predictions for 2025 and all new mega trufaxual predictions for 2026

On  By [KPOPALYPSE](https://kpopalypse.com/author/kpopalypse/)

It’s time for Kpopalypse to look back on [his predictions for 2025](https://kpopalypse.com/2024/12/14/results-of-kpopalypses-predictions-for-2024-and-all-new-ultra-trufaxual-predictions-for-2025/), and make all new predictions for 2026! Come with Kpopalypse for a look into the crystal ball of k-pop!

Before we delve into the incredible revelations of 2026, let’s take a look at 2025 – at around this time last year I made a lot of bold predictions with my notorious super Boram ESP powers! How well did Kpopalypse do?

\*

## RESULTS OF 2025 PREDICTIONS

**The big winner of the NewJeans/HYBE battle is NOBODY** – CORRECT. NewJeans [lost the court cases that mattered](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cy5q0leq2lpo), reluctantly returning to HYBE subsidiary ADOR with their tails between their legs and without their creative director/manipulator/sixth member of the group/[Kpopalypse bias](https://kpopalypse.com/2024/04/24/positive-post-min-hee-jin-ador-ceo/) Min Hee Jin to “there-there” them into compliance. HYBE in the meantime lost all the fire and momentum they had invested into a group that was clearly on a trajectory to have BTS-levels of pop-culture impact, and now their only options are releasing more comebacks from the poisoned well that fans will forever now see as “forced”, or just putting them on ice until contract expiry.

**Meanwhile, people will finally stop copying NewJeans… just kidding LOL no they won’t** – CORRECT. Almost every single girl group, and [even a few of the boy groups](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gb_VcDoxa20), went all-in on NewJeansification in a desperate bid to try and snatch some of that untapped market now that HYBE fumbled the bag. Light fluffy uptempo beats, wispy pitch-corrected melodies, slightly retro and deliberately badly-lit VHS videos with collage elements and colourful plushie-stapling cut-up fashions were everywhere. There was nothing “[super shy](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ArmDp-zijuc)” about NewJeans’ influence, but there’s a big cultural void here and so far nobody either within or outside of HYBE’s roster has been able to convincingly Fillit.

**The ‘shout’ chorus trend starts finally decreasing** – CORRECT. While some of the nugus are still doing it, the groups that actually matter have veered off into different directions.

**Rock remains big** – CORRECT. Rock had a big year, and not just with actual rock groups, even big idol group names were adding more distortion into their mixes and strapping on guitars to flex their rock credentials. Of course the songs still mostly sucked anyway, but at least everyone looked cooler.

**Lots of duets** – CORRECT. While nobody really tried copying the *sound* of Rose and Bruno Mars’ “[APT](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ekr2nIex040)” (because heaven forbid k-pop agencies copy an upbeat pop song that actually sounds fun and works instead of some lame bullshit), a lot of people saw a western person and a k-pop person on a track together and thought “hmmm, profit”. Of course with everyone missing the point, actual profits were minimal, but the effort was definitely there as we were spammed endless duets between big k-pop artists and slightly-less-big westerners doing their best to ride the coattails.

**Veteran groups do well, newbies struggle** – CORRECT. Nobody is listening to your favourite C-list group. Not yet, anyway.

**Videos with AI content start to suck slightly less** – CORRECT. Previous to 2025, only boomers were fooled into thinking that AI visual effects looked good, but now even garden variety k-pop fan morons with reasonable technological proficiency really thought that 100 Blackpink fans would stand against a brick wall and bash their heads against if if their bias told them to… okay that’s a bad analogy, because they totally would, but you know what I mean.

**More problems with ‘personal relations’ in the music business** – CORRECT. This year it was the boys’ turn to be exploited and mistreated, with groups like [Fantasy Boys](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sqlbbgm2bJ4) and [Omega X](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-05-22/national/socialAffairs/Former-executive-of-Omega-Xs-label-charged-with-sexual-harassment/2313374) feeling the brunt of questionable management practices. But don’t worry girl group fans, I’m sure your faves aren’t being left out and we’ll find out all about their heartaches in 2026.

**The Han Seo Hee saga continues** – CORRECT. Between [slapping down the ex-head of YG in court battles](https://entertainment.inquirer.net/619800/yg-head-yang-hyun-suk-gets-6-month-suspended-sentence-for-coercion) (the NewJeans girls could learn a thing or two here from a true court fashionista) and [spreading rumours about your bias just for the fun of tormeting fandom idiots](https://kbizoom.com/han-seo-hee-false-idol-rumors-exposed/), Han Seo Hee had a typically active year.

**Idols get more risque on social media** – [CORRECT](https://www.instagram.com/u_youha/?hl=en). One of the big shifts caused by the influence of “global girl groups” with more relaxed skin exposure rules, plus Blackpink’s more recent live stages designed with global rather than Korean audiences in mind, is that the needle for what’s acceptable k-pop fashion is moving ever-so-slightly back in the 2015 direction. Watch out, there could be more Kpopalypse fashion class posts coming in 2026!

**Coming out** – CORRECT. I predicted people in k-pop to come out this year and boy did 2025 deliver. So far we’ve had [Lara from Katseye coming out as queer](https://www.teenvogue.com/story/katseye-member-lara-raj-came-out-as-queer-to-fans-on-weverse-im-half-fruitcake) and [XG’s Cocona as transmasc non-binary](https://www.billboard.com/music/music-news/xg-cocona-comes-out-transmasculine-non-binary-1236130004/). However if you’re one of those fussy people who don’t consider something k-pop unless the performers have “Made in Korea” stamped on their agency punchcard, we also had coming-outs from [JWiver’s Ryujin (now Cherry)](https://www.asianjunkie.com/2025/07/22/cherry-formerly-jwiivers-ryujin-comes-out-as-trans-talks-about-finding-happiness-after-idol-life/), [Krystian Wang of Boys II Planet](https://www.reddit.com/r/kpop/comments/1m9dxvb/boys_ii_planet_contestant_krystian_wang_has_come/), and [Bain of Just B](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cd6jzd6g9nwo). This is an unprecedented shift for a scene known previously for keeping up appearances of total gender heteronormativity (aside from the odd male-on-male fanservice/BGP). Remember that you read it first at Kpopalypse.com, *before* it happened.

**SM has a good year compared to other big agencies** – CORRECT. Absolutely no issues in the aespa camp this year as they kept on keeping on, raising their popularity significantly. I’ve heard that SM’s boy groups did well too but I couldn’t catch any details of the news around it above the sound of all my gay male readers fapping.

**Cultural education remains a low priority** – CORRECT. Kiss Of Life [partied a little too hard](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-04-08/entertainment/kpop/Kiss-of-Life-controversy-reignites-debate-over-cultural-appropriation-in-Kpop/2280311) for the likes of many international k-pop fans, who asked that the group please stop enjoying black culture so much. Of course [they weren’t the only ones](https://x.com/vegetariantrex_/status/1986837773920108652) to make some cultural missteps this year, but they *were* attractive women, which meant that people actually noticed and cared.

**Music chart scandals come back** – INCORRECT. The only complaining about charts this year was just corporations [having a whinge that they weren’t making as much money as usual](https://www.theguardian.com/music/2025/mar/27/can-k-pop-overcome-crisis)… and then whinging again about being overshadowed by Kpop Demon Hunters.

**Kpopalypse Interview returns** – CORRECT. K-pop website [kpopping.com](https://kpopalypse.com/2025/04/11/kpopalypse-interview-kpopping/) took the plunge and consented to a Kpopalypse interview, proving that the caonima spirit is still alive in the kpoponlineosphere! Who knew that other k-pop websites were this brave? You can thank them by [remembering Aileegate](https://kpopalypse.com/2025/11/14/the-second-kpopalypse-allkpop-performance-review/).

**Song quality will stay consistent** – CORRECT, unfortunately, in this case it just meant consistently shit. Consistently shit is still consistent though, so I’ll take the win.

**Despite getting flak for anti-feminist sentiment, QWER members continue to pop up in unexpected places that would suggest a more pro-feminist stance and this confuses the fuck out of everybody except Kpopalypse. Eventually something forces the issue and it’s revealed that QWER is actually a pro-feminist sleeper cell designed to target Korea’s right-wing gamer incels by the dopamine receptors and make them more amenable to girls who can rock, while taking their money of course** – CORRECT. QWER’s parent agency 3Y Corporation was recently revealed to be the company behind the game “[Five Hearts Under One Roof](https://store.steampowered.com/app/3021100/Five_Hearts_Under_One_Roof/)“, a live-action “dating simulator” but *without* the usual adult content that almost all these games contain, where your goal is to *behave respectfully* among five women that you’re boarding with so they don’t hate you. Being an asshole is not allowed, only pure feminist thoughts, or you lose the game and have to start over. QWER forcing incel gamer nerds to behave like true feminist allies in order to fill their achievement bars, now if that isn’t a feminist sleeper cell targeting gamers in action I don’t know what is.

**Kpopalypse will continue to be a cunt** – CORRECT. What else is new?

---

So as usual Kpopalypse predictions were mostly correct thanks once again to SUPER BORAM ESP POWERS – [Michael Shermer](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8T_jwq9ph8k) feast on the dried poostains stuck to my asscrack! Now it’s time to see what Kpopalypse forsees for 2026! Super Boram ESP powers are now activated!

\*

## KPOPALYPSE’S PREDICTIONS FOR 2026

**NewJeans come back – but you’ll wish they hadn’t** – HYBE try to claw back some ROI from the millions they’ve sunk into NewJeans but the girls don’t give a fuck anymore, neither do their support staff, and everyone involved puts in the absolute bare minimum of effort. The result is some total bullshit that looks and sounds like a stand-in NewJeans clone rather than NewJeans themselves, while actual stand-in NewJeans clone Illit continue to play nicer with the same bosses and get rewarded with the better songs.

**BTS board the fail train** – the first full group song from BTS after their miltiary hiatus is shockingly bad. After the breakout success of Kpop Demon Hunters, new k-pop fans expected better than this from a supposed legacy group.

**But gosh those Kpop Demon Hunters singers do alright** – you can expect Ejae, Rei Ami and Audrey Nuna to keep turning up all over the place… and some of the songs won’t suck, either.

**YG finally start getting BabyMonster right** – after about 57 failed experiments at everything under the sun, YG Entertainment finally work out what they’re doing and point BabyMonster in some sort of vaguely consistent musical direction.

**JYP crosses the streams of k-pop and religion** – frustrated that his wacky church YouTube sermons aren’t getting enough views, JYP starts insisting on more slightly-under-the-radar-but-not-too-much religious content in the products of his agency. I hope you’re ready for JYPE The Grace.

**LGBT is here to stay** – now that the initial doors have been knocked down and nobody’s career died from the impact, you can expect regular coming-outs from k-pop alumni as just part of the normal k-pop landscape now.

**AI song controversies ahoy** – if you think the use of AI in k-pop concept art and music videos is a concern, just wait until an agency gets caught using AI to write their entire artist catalog.

**Rock gradually goes out of style again** – 2025 mainly just taught k-pop agencies that none of them know how to actually write a decent rock song, so during 2026 they’ll gradually wind down their attempts at milking a culture they don’t understand and have their artists go back to sitting politely in wooden chairs in front of flower arrangements or whatever it is that people who are out of their depth making rock music think a good use of their artists’ time is.

**Boy groups improve** – with BTS’s legacy waning, there’s room for a new competitior to step up to the plate. Don’t expect the same level of commercial success with these ventures, but we might just get a banger or two along the way.

**Idols continue to enjoy culture, including the cultures that they shouldn’t** – k-pop fans, just like the KKK and neo-Nazis, love racial segregation and keeping foreign influences as far away from their precious Korean music as possible. Better hope your bias shaves those braids off before their next comeback. Everyone wants to be Norwegian, but nobody wants to *be* Norwegian.

**A relatively good year for k-pop comebacks** – well it couldn’t get that much worse than 2025, could it.

**Another JAV/k-pop connection surfaces** – and you’ll probably hear it here first. You can always trust [Kpopalypse.com](https://kpopalypse.com/) to report on this type of important cultural crossover, that’s because the healing of Korea/Japan tensions means a lot to me.

**Kpopalypse gains some “different” attention** – what could it be? I look forward to finding out!

**Han Seo Hee has a quiet year** – this is the boldest prediction on this list.

**Asian Junkie posts again** – actually no, *this* is the boldest prediction on this list. He may even finally get his favourite songs of 2024 list published.

**Fifty Fifty’s “[Starry Night](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lzm-7YfBJBU)” remains a reasonably good song** – you could listen to it, if you wanted.

**Noting the success of Kpop Demon Hunters, an agency decides that the reason why the songs were successful is the high-flying vocals of “[Golden](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yebNIHKAC4A)” so they create a concept just to mimic that specific type of vocal sound. Of course, nobody within a 10km radius of the agency building can actually sing like that, but that doesn’t stop the agency training up some attractive people a little in the hope of eventually striking vocal gold. However everything gets undermined when it’s revealed that not only is the song mid as fuck compared to “Golden” but the group in question are average singers even after their years of idol training and had to resort to using the same computer alteration tricks that everybody else is** – this year’s hyperspecific prediction is aimed at the cyncism of those who doubt my Super Boram ESP powers!

**Kpopalypse will continue to annoy cunts by being an even bigger cunt, cunt** – you know it’s true.

---

That’s all the predictions for the next 12 months! My final prediction is that Kpopalypse will return soon with more posts!

# K-Pop Entertainment Industry Statistics

K-pop sits at 23.6% of South Korea’s music subscriptions by 2021, yet the wider industry momentum keeps accelerating, from KRW 19.8 trillion in advertising spending to the global esports audience hitting 532.8 million in 2024. If you want to understand why fandom behavior is spreading across streaming, live shows, creator deals, and even mobile games, this page stitches the key signals into one place, including the first 8 weeks where 38% of K-pop consumption concentrates after release.

Written by Christopher Lee·Edited by Lucia Mendez·Fact-checked by Jason Clarke

··Next review Nov 2026 [...] ··Next review Nov 2026

 Editorially verified
 Independent research
 21 sources
 Verified 12 May 2026

### Key Statistics

15highlights from this report

1 / 15

> K-pop made up 23.6% of all music subscriptions in South Korea’s top music markets by 2021 (reported by Deezer’s global K-pop analysis)

> In 2023, the number of K-pop fan events in Japan grew by 22% year-on-year (tour/fan event dataset summarized by a trade publication)

> In 2022, the number of K-pop-related shows licensed in Southeast Asia increased by 19% (KOCCA content distribution tracking)

> The South Korean music market was valued at 11.2 trillion KRW in 2023, up 12.4% year-on-year

> The global K-pop merchandise market was $2.31 billion in 2023 and is projected to reach $3.62 billion by 2030 [...] Verified

### Cost Analysis – Interpretation

In 2023, South Korea’s advertising market hit KRW 19.8 trillion while influencer marketing was already heavily shaped by brand deals and creator partnerships at 43% of budgets, and these high promotion and partnership costs help explain why K-pop trainee systems can demand 2,800+ training hours to produce debut ready talent despite the industry employing 466,000 people in arts, entertainment, and recreation in 2022.

# K-Pop Entertainment Industry Statistics

K-pop sits at 23.6% of South Korea’s music subscriptions by 2021, yet the wider industry momentum keeps accelerating, from KRW 19.8 trillion in advertising spending to the global esports audience hitting 532.8 million in 2024. If you want to understand why fandom behavior is spreading across streaming, live shows, creator deals, and even mobile games, this page stitches the key signals into one place, including the first 8 weeks where 38% of K-pop consumption concentrates after release.

Written by [Christopher Lee](/about/christopher-lee/)·Edited by [Lucia Mendez](/about/lucia-mendez/)·Fact-checked by [Jason Clarke](/about/jason-clarke/)

··Next review Nov 2026

* Editorially verified
* Independent research
* 21 sources
* Verified 12 May 2026

### Key Statistics

15highlights from this report

1 / 15

> K-pop made up 23.6% of all music subscriptions in South Korea’s top music markets by 2021 (reported by Deezer’s global K-pop analysis)

> In 2023, the number of K-pop fan events in Japan grew by 22% year-on-year (tour/fan event dataset summarized by a trade publication)

> In 2022, the number of K-pop-related shows licensed in Southeast Asia increased by 19% (KOCCA content distribution tracking)

> The South Korean music market was valued at 11.2 trillion KRW in 2023, up 12.4% year-on-year

> The global K-pop merchandise market was $2.31 billion in 2023 and is projected to reach $3.62 billion by 2030

> The global music streaming market reached $78.0 billion in 2023

> In 2023, Netflix reported 260.3 million paid memberships globally (context for K-pop-related shows’ discoverability)

> In 2024, the number of global esports viewers reached 532.8 million (Newzoo global estimate) indicating overlapping fandom behavior with K-pop

> In 2022, K-pop content accounted for 17% of time spent on Korean entertainment video platforms among young audiences (media study summary in a journal article)

> In 2023, BTS’s ‘Butter’ earned 780 million YouTube views within 6 months after release (views reported in YouTube/BTS milestone coverage)

> KOCCA reported that the average global fanbase growth rate for K-content in 2022 was 11.7% (KOCCA annual KPI figure summarized in Korea Creative Content Agency releases)

> 12.7% of global touring revenue in 2023 was generated by pop acts (relevance: K-pop sits largely within the pop category on tour economics)

> In 2023, South Korea’s advertising market spending was KRW 19.8 trillion (Korea’s media/advertising statistics used for promotion cost baselines)

> In 2022, South Korea’s employment in ‘arts, entertainment, and recreation’ was 466,000 people (ILO modelled estimate cited by ILOSTAT)

> In 2022, brand deals and creator partnerships accounted for 43% of influencer marketing budgets globally (Influencer Marketing Hub estimate)

## Key Takeaways

K-pop is driving booming streaming, merchandise, touring, and global fan growth, capturing 23.6% of subscriptions in Korea by 2021.

* 1

  K-pop made up 23.6% of all music subscriptions in South Korea’s top music markets by 2021 (reported by Deezer’s global K-pop analysis)
* 2

  In 2023, the number of K-pop fan events in Japan grew by 22% year-on-year (tour/fan event dataset summarized by a trade publication)
* 3

  In 2022, the number of K-pop-related shows licensed in Southeast Asia increased by 19% (KOCCA content distribution tracking)
* 4

  The South Korean music market was valued at 11.2 trillion KRW in 2023, up 12.4% year-on-year
* 5

  The global K-pop merchandise market was $2.31 billion in 2023 and is projected to reach $3.62 billion by 2030
* 6

  The global music streaming market reached $78.0 billion in 2023
* 7

  In 2023, Netflix reported 260.3 million paid memberships globally (context for K-pop-related shows’ discoverability)
* 8

  In 2024, the number of global esports viewers reached 532.8 million (Newzoo global estimate) indicating overlapping fandom behavior with K-pop
* 9

  In 2022, K-pop content accounted for 17% of time spent on Korean entertainment video platforms among young audiences (media study summary in a journal article)
* 10

  In 2023, BTS’s ‘Butter’ earned 780 million YouTube views within 6 months after release (views reported in YouTube/BTS milestone coverage)
* 11

  KOCCA reported that the average global fanbase growth rate for K-content in 2022 was 11.7% (KOCCA annual KPI figure summarized in Korea Creative Content Agency releases)
* 12

  12.7% of global touring revenue in 2023 was generated by pop acts (relevance: K-pop sits largely within the pop category on tour economics)
* 13

  In 2023, South Korea’s advertising market spending was KRW 19.8 trillion (Korea’s media/advertising statistics used for promotion cost baselines)
* 14

  In 2022, South Korea’s employment in ‘arts, entertainment, and recreation’ was 466,000 people (ILO modelled estimate cited by ILOSTAT)
* 15

  In 2022, brand deals and creator partnerships accounted for 43% of influencer marketing budgets globally (Influencer Marketing Hub estimate)

Independently sourced · editorially reviewed

## How we built this report

Every data point in this report goes through a four-stage verification process:

1. 01

   ### Primary source collection

   Our research team aggregates data from peer-reviewed studies, official statistics, industry reports, and longitudinal studies. Only sources with disclosed methodology and sample sizes are eligible.
2. 02

   ### Editorial curation and exclusion

   An editor reviews collected data and excludes figures from non-transparent surveys, outdated or unreplicated studies, and samples below significance thresholds. Only data that passes this filter enters verification.
3. 03

   ### Independent verification

   Each statistic is checked via reproduction analysis, cross-referencing against independent sources, or modelling where applicable. We verify the claim, not just cite it.
4. 04

   ### Human editorial cross-check

   Only statistics that pass verification are eligible for publication. A human editor reviews results, handles edge cases, and makes the final inclusion decision.

Statistics that could not be independently verified are excluded. Confidence labels use an editorial target distribution of roughly 70% Verified, 15% Directional, and 15% Single source (assigned deterministically per statistic).

K-pop is still reshaping how people spend on music and culture in real time, and the latest figures are bigger than most fans expect. In 2023, the South Korean music market hit 11.2 trillion KRW with a 12.4% year on year jump, while 38% of K-pop listening clustered in just the first eight weeks after release. Once you connect streaming windows, merchandising growth, and licensing economics, you start to see why the K-pop entertainment industry behaves less like a single hit and more like a tightly timed global machine.

# K-Culture Trends 2026: K-Pop, Music Industry, and AI

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

[Yunhwa E. Koh, PhD](https://kr.linkedin.com/in/yunhwakoh?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)

[New Publication: K-Culture Trends 2026] I’m happy to share the release of K-Culture Trends 2026, the latest annual volume published by the K-Culture Trends Research Institute, to which I contributed as a co-author for the fourth consecutive year since 2023. This book presents a collective analysis of contemporary K-culture, spanning K-pop and popular music, as well as films, drama, television entertainment, and webtoons. Through interdisciplinary perspectives, we examine how cultural practices and industry structures are being reshaped in a rapidly changing social and global environment. In this edition, I particularly contributed to the music section, focusing on how the rapid development of generative AI is transforming the music industry — from production and creativity to labor, authorship, and industrial organization. My aim was to situate these technological shifts within the broader dynamics of K-pop and the Korean music ecosystem, rather than treating AI simply as a technical trend. For me, this ongoing project has been an effort to document K-culture not as a passing phenomenon, but as a dynamic cultural field shaped by tensions between technology, industry, and creative practice. Collaborating with fellow researchers each year continues to be both intellectually stimulating and deeply rewarding. I hope this volume can serve as a useful reference for scholars, students, and practitioners interested in understanding the current landscape and future directions of K-culture. More details: [https://lnkd.in/gbeNCVus](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FgbeNCVus&urlhash=tU1F&trk=public_post-text) My sincere thanks to all the co-authors and the institute for another meaningful collaboration. title: K-pop in 2026: Straight to the Source
content:
Image 6

To be clear, not every act is using this formula. ATEEZ and IVE’s continued success this year proves that traditional intro-verse-chorus structure still delivers, Hearts2Hearts captures the “house” approach to a legacy K-pop formula perfected by SM Entertainment, and YENA’s retro-throwback sound suggests there’s even an appetite for an older K-pop aesthetic altogether. But the trend worth watching is the straight-to-the-point approach, and it says a lot about where ears and attention spans are heading in 2026. [...] What's striking about 2026 is that K-pop’s biggest acts seem to have skipped the hybrid altogether. After years of K-pop absorbing these genres from a distance, the superstars are now going straight to the source — and the results are some of the most interesting music either artist has ever released.

Of the major 2026 releases, I found BLACKPINK’s “GO”5 the most intriguing. It is more reminiscent of an authentic techno record than what we’d typically expect from the K-pop industry. The chorus replaces a catchy hook with a classic EDM drop, a structure also used in their single “JUMP” (produced by Diplo) last year.

Video 6 [...] The hip-hop and EDM influences were always there. What’s different in 2026 is the confidence, and therefore the willingness to stop filtering those influences through a K-pop lens and just let them exist on their own terms. Whether that’s a sign of the genre maturing, its audiences maturing, or both, I’m not sure. But it’s worth paying attention to.

And if my morning commute playlist is any indication, it sounds pretty good either way. I’m just happy we’re getting more music!

2026 is shaping up to be one of the most remarkable years in K-pop history. EXO, TWICE, BLACKPINK, BTS, and BIGBANG are all active within the same year. This is a convergence of titans that hasn’t happened before at this scale. And yet, the most striking thing about their returns isn’t the fact that they’re happening. It’s what they sound like.

Because increasingly, a lot of them don’t sound like K-pop. Or what we’d expected.[1](https://wooski.substack.com/p/k-pop-in-2026-straight-to-the-source#footnote-1)

**If there are two genres I listen to on a daily basis, it’s hip-hop and EDM.** The beats keep me going through my morning and evening commutes. My Spotify playlists run deep in both worlds. What’s been striking me lately is how seamlessly my K-pop listens have been bleeding into those same playlists. [Nearly every K-pop act charting high right now](https://www.melon.com/chart/index.htm) (in March 2026) is pulling from hip-hop or EDM in some form, and the superstar comeback class of 2026 is no exception.

Here I want to map out what’s actually happening sonically — the virality mechanics, the genre influences, and what it means when the biggest names in K-pop don’t really sound like K-pop at all.

## Virality and Repetition

There’s a specific trend I want to point out regarding keywords.

Take a listen (if you haven’t already) to hot rookie girl group KiiiKiii’s “404 (New Era)”, released January 2026:

[Video 1](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zhHB4dZTChw)

As you can tell, there’s a distinct sound here. We can conveniently categorize this as “house” — a subgenre of EDM — and “UK House” / “Garage” if we go even further. It’s the type of music you hear at modern apparel stores like Zara or Aritzia; the audience feels chic and nonchalant while bobbing their heads in the consistent rhythm of “_404, the new era, era._”

But how often does KiiiKiii _actually say_ “404” verbatim?

The song has 4 choruses, each with 8 “404”s and 7 more times outside the chorus. **That’s a whopping 39 “404”s sung in total**. No wonder the track is instantly catchy, because our brains get hooked on a single keyword that repeats throughout!

Take another example: boy band ENHYPEN’s “Knife” also released in January 2026:

[Video 2](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=R6e4tBWxIxE)

The keyword here? You guessed it - “_Knife_”.

“Knife” is on a completely different sonic lane, based on the “rage”-type hip-hop sound popularized by artists like Travis Scott and Playboi Carti. This is another banger that I start my day with. **The choruses alone contain 29 “**_**Knife!**_**”s and 2 more outside, for a total of 31.** The track is only 2:19 long on ENHYPEN’s latest LP; in fact, 5 of the 11 songs are under 2 minutes, and 10 of those 11 are under 3.

[Share](https://wooski.substack.com/p/k-pop-in-2026-straight-to-the-source?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&action=share)

One last example takes a slightly different approach: LNGSHOT’s “Moonwalkin’”, from January 2026:

[Video 3](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HJgdT15UT4k)

Unlike the two examples above, the focus here isn’t necessarily the title keyword, but the **specific melody and rhythm** that kicks off the chorus - “시간은 계속 tick-tockin / _Siganeun gyesok tick-tockin_…”. **The actual word “Moonwalkin’” isn’t repeated in the same mantra-like way as “404” or “Knife”.** Instead, the **pre-chorus seamlessly blends into the chorus**, avoiding a traditional, delayed climax.

This chorus-first structure, although it existed throughout the years, is worth its own observation again today. **Rather than waiting up to a minute for a build-up to the chorus as in more traditional K-pop, we're dropped into the hook immediately and invited to pay attention.**The same approach appears in recent hip-hop based K-pop tracks such as ALLDAY PROJECT’s “[FAMOUS](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VjvzYjU1mY0&list=RDVjvzYjU1mY0&start_radio=1)” (2025) and CORTIS’s “[GO!](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WXS-o57VJ5w&list=RDWXS-o57VJ5w&start_radio=1)” (2025), both of which open by emphasizing a specific section and rhythm over a long build-up. Here’s an easy comparison chart:

[![Image 6](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!758R!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F45070d4b-d977-49dc-a394-7d4a4511c6d5_875x590.png)](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!758R!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F45070d4b-d977-49dc-a394-7d4a4511c6d5_875x590.png)

To be clear, not every act is using this formula. [ATEEZ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vqkfEUqjl6Y&list=RDvqkfEUqjl6Y&start_radio=1&pp=ygUFYXRlZXqgBwE%3D) and [IVE’s continued success](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9qkpcLK422o&list=RD9qkpcLK422o&start_radio=1) this year proves that traditional intro-verse-chorus structure still delivers, [Hearts2Hearts captures the “house” approach](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F7sGJVUrkjQ&list=RDF7sGJVUrkjQ&start_radio=1) to a legacy K-pop formula perfected by SM Entertainment, and [YENA’s retro-throwback sound](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ItSKahBISg0&list=RDItSKahBISg0&start_radio=1) suggests there’s even an appetite for an older K-pop aesthetic altogether. But the trend worth watching is the straight-to-the-point approach, and it says a lot about where ears and attention spans are heading in 2026.

Are we being given shorter soundbites and punchline keywords because of our dwindling attention spans? [Songs sound shorter today](https://hmc.chartmetric.com/shorter-songs-trend-streaming-history/) than they were years ago - the average charting song nowadays is 17 seconds shorter than in 2018, and by _29 seconds_ for hip-hop! Whether this is driven by the current genre trends or our desire for more dopamine is worth studying. What’s clear is that all three songs are engineered for:

*   [Short-form content and virality](https://newsroom.tiktok.com/tiktok-reveals-the-top-artists-and-songs-of-2025?lang=en),

*   Catchy, repeatable dance choreography, and

*   A repetitive structure that aligns seamlessly with standard K-pop marketing strategies.

## Genre-Driven Convergence

I’ve used the three songs above as examples to illustrate an interesting phenomenon in the Korean music industry right now — a move towards more genre-straightforward sounds that naturally draw from what’s trending in their American counterparts. But to see the full picture, we need to look at the ecosystem outside of just K-pop.

#### K-Hiphop Updates

The past few years have been strange for Korean hip-hop (“K-hiphop”). Korea’s premier rap competition show, _Show Me The Money_ (“SMTM”)[2](https://wooski.substack.com/p/k-pop-in-2026-straight-to-the-source#footnote-2), went on hiatus for 3 years due to [decreased mainstream interest in hip-hop and viewership](https://www.yna.co.kr/view/AKR20221231019700005). Ironically, this mirrors the American industry as [hip-hop is no longer as hot worldwide as it used to be](https://www.cnn.com/2025/10/30/entertainment/rap-falls-out-top-40).

Yet without SMTM dominating the K-hiphop conversation, 2025 turned out to be one of the more innovative years for K-hiphop after a few years of post-COVID stagnation. I’m currently enjoying SMTM 12 in 2026, even if the general hype around me isn’t what it used to be. It’s great to see the next generation of talent in the group [OKASHII](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cOYOc0YwDCk&list=RDcOYOc0YwDCk&start_radio=1) (Jeffrey White, Mason Home, Raf Sandou), the solidification of [ZENE THE ZILLA](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XNDiTp9-_78&list=RDXNDiTp9-_78&start_radio=1) as a major player, and the recognition of the scene-stealer [Marv](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4LE97XeM6yI&list=RD4LE97XeM6yI&start_radio=1).

Aside from the show, rapper Sik-K — previously under Jay Park’s H1GHR Music — has now cemented his status as the top dog with his own label, KC. His collaborative album <[K-FLIP+](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3FRwcncgIvY&list=RD3FRwcncgIvY&start_radio=1)> with veteran producer Lil Moshpit (better known as Hwi-min of GroovyRoom) swept almost every major hip-hop award, even the _Best Rap & Hip-Hop Album_ at the prestigious _23rd Korean Music Awards_ (“한국대중음악상”).[3](https://wooski.substack.com/p/k-pop-in-2026-straight-to-the-source#footnote-3)

[Video 4](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MDEok6_kx6U)

The album experiments heavily with [sampling](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-WEQi54xZTk&list=RD-WEQi54xZTk&start_radio=1) (drawing _mostly_ from _Korean_ rock, dance, and hip-hop songs, which is unusual) and leans into the rage hip-hop sound. We’ve been hearing similar influences in K-pop boy bands recently, such as the aforementioned “Knife”, CORTIS’s “[FASHION](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=42wfEs7oIP8&list=RD42wfEs7oIP8&start_radio=1&pp=ygUOY29ydGlzIGZhc2hpb26gBwE%3D)”, and RIIZE’s “[Fame](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TVqEwvbZP30&list=RDTVqEwvbZP30&start_radio=1&pp=ygUKcmlpemUgZmFtZaAHAQ%3D%3D)”.

But the K-hiphop rebound wasn’t just limited to rage; in fact it involved dubstep. Effie (although she’s active been for over 5 years) was recognized as _Best New Artist_ at the _2025 Korean Hiphop Awards_, with strong strong recognition from global outlets including [The New York Times](https://www.nytimes.com/2025/12/05/arts/music/best-albums-2025.html) and [Pitchfork](https://pitchfork.com/reviews/albums/effie-pullup-to-busan-4-more-hyper-summer-its-gonna-be-a-fuckin-movie-ep/). Her EPs stay true to her hyperpop identity, a sound largely shaped by her producer [kimj](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HlsbRhi-mD4) who skillfully builds on the dubstep influences that Skrillex had popularized.[4](https://wooski.substack.com/p/k-pop-in-2026-straight-to-the-source#footnote-4)

[Video 5](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sZxLJLj9o38)

The musical boundary between K-pop and hip-hop has long been contested. Two scenes once considered [bitter enemies](https://www.scmp.com/lifestyle/entertainment/article/3019143/rapper-b-free-says-sorry-bts-slamming-them-interview-six) are increasingly indistinguishable. [Many rappers](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_eS7FhYCyVo&list=RD_eS7FhYCyVo&start_radio=1) who started their careers [as K-pop idols](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=da2SVbzOI7Q&list=RDda2SVbzOI7Q&start_radio=1), or those still [primarily associated](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qsUA0w6uYEE&list=RDqsUA0w6uYEE&start_radio=1) with K-pop, [now move fluidly](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ChAXAFqzBpw&list=RDChAXAFqzBpw&start_radio=1) between hip-hop and mainstream pop without much friction. The lines have been blurring for years, but in 2026 I feel they’ve dissolved further. Although at the end of the day on this debate, I think they all look to the same direction. Any Korean rendition of hip-hop inevitably draws from the West, and ultimately Black musicians that originated those influences.

A lot of current K-pop idol tracks now sound close to K-hiphop, and in turn to the original hip-hop and EDM sounds coming out of the West, that **perhaps K-pop may be moving away from building hybrid sounds from scratch — and toward importing and refining what those scenes have already proven.**

#### The Superstar Direction

K-pop has long been building on the hip-hop and EDM sounds to build something distinctly its own. But we may run into a chicken-or-the-egg parable. **Did K-pop's global dominance give its artists the freedom to go genre-purist, or did the audiences evolve first, pulling the artists toward more authentic sounds?**

What's striking about 2026 is that K-pop’s biggest acts seem to have skipped the hybrid altogether. After years of K-pop absorbing these genres from a distance, the superstars are now going straight to the source — and the results are some of the most interesting music either artist has ever released.

Of the major 2026 releases, I found BLACKPINK’s “[GO](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2GJfWMYCWY0)”[5](https://wooski.substack.com/p/k-pop-in-2026-straight-to-the-source#footnote-5) the most intriguing. It is more reminiscent of an authentic techno record than what we’d typically expect from the K-pop industry. The chorus replaces a catchy hook with a classic EDM drop, a structure also used in their single “[JUMP](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CgCVZdcKcqY&list=RDCgCVZdcKcqY&start_radio=1&pp=ygUOYmxhY2twaW5rIGp1bXCgBwE%3D)” (produced by Diplo) last year.

[Video 6](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2GJfWMYCWY0)

“GO” and “JUMP” actually remind me of K-pop in the early 2010s, when it was heavily shaped by the golden era of EDM: a buildup, catchphrase (ex: “[Wow, Fantastic Baby](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AAbokV76tkU&list=RDAAbokV76tkU&start_radio=1&pp=ygUOZmFudGFzdGljIGJhYnmgBwE%3D)”), and a drop that hits like a freight train. There’s something nostalgic and cathartic about hearing that formula executed at this level in 2026.

I agree with Korean pop critics Kim Do Heon and Jang Jun Hwan, as discussed on their channel [ZENERATE](https://www.youtube.com/@ZENERATEMUSIC), that [BLACKPINK’s “Banger Era”](https://youtu.be/c4ho0FNe0xM?si=2zw-w5103BALqRzx&t=778) may be underway. There is a boldness to the sound experimentation here — a charisma that only artists at this level can pull off — reminiscent of BTS's "ON" (2020).

This brings us to BTS’s long-awaited return. The first track off their new album _ARIRANG_ (2026), "Body to Body", is rooted far more in hip-hop than mainstream pop:

[Video 7](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RBaSiVjtKR4)

_ARIRANG_ as a whole adopts a heavy, beat-focused direction [(with nods to 808s and jersey club](https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/lists/bts-arirang-track-by-track-breakdown/like-animals/)) featuring credits from Mike WiLL-Made It, Flume, Diplo, and JPEGMAFIA. The decision to circle back to BTS’s original identity as a hip-hop group seems to have caught many by surprise, as the songs are quite different from the vibrant, colorful, mainstream pop of their pre-hiatus era. The beats here, including the title track “SWIM”, take a far more [subtle approach to storytelling](https://www.forbes.com/sites/hannahabraham/2026/03/20/mature-ferocious-uneven-bts-arirang-reviews-are-in/).

While I won’t dissect _ARIRANG_ in detail here, I think there’s also something worth sitting with regarding the “Korean-ness” of _ARIRANG_‘s direction — how BTS chose to convey that identity without leaning on the overtly Korean melodic references of a track like “[IDOL](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MXFkjMNXfpY&list=RDMXFkjMNXfpY&start_radio=1)” (2018). It’s a subtler, more internalized kind of cultural expression, and it deserves its own deeper look.

For now, what both BLACKPINK and BTS illustrate is **something only superstar artists can afford: genuine genre experimentation.** The decision to deliberately step away from the hybrid “K-pop” sound, and lean into authentic techno or original hip-hop, may puzzle some audiences. **But it also underscores how difficult it is to box in artists whose global, public personas are perceived as synonymous with K-pop itself.**

At this level, star power becomes the buffer. It’s what allows BLACKPINK to release straightforward techno, BTS to pursue their hip-hop origins, and other top acts to **defy the very expectations that made them globally recognizable in the first place.**

The answer to that chicken-or-the-egg parable may simply be that **the genres themselves are everywhere, in broader Korean popular music as a whole**. Rage, house, hyperpop, techno, and jersey club are no longer trends to borrow from cautiously.**They're ingrained across the entire spectrum**, from KiiiKiii, Effie, to BLACKPINK, from LNGSHOT, Sik-K, to BTS.

#### **So perhaps at the very top in any scene, there are no so-called genres. Just artists.**

## Closing Thoughts

I realize that the word “K-pop” sometimes gets more complicated than it seems. Depending on who you ask, it conjures entirely different sounds, eras, and artists. This gap only widens as K-pop evolves.

For what it’s worth, I’ve never thought of K-pop as a fixed sound. **It’s a constantly changing, flexible idea — a global cultural phenomenon and a business model that borrows, adapts, and reinvents rather than one that restricts what an artist can or can’t do.** What I’ve tried to map out here is one snapshot of that reinvention in real time.

The hip-hop and EDM influences were always there. **What’s different in 2026 is the confidence, and therefore the willingness to stop filtering those influences through a K-pop lens and just let them exist on their own terms.** Whether that’s a sign of the genre maturing, its audiences maturing, or both, I’m not sure. But it’s worth paying attention to.

And if my morning commute playlist is any indication, it sounds pretty good either way. I’m just happy we’re getting more music!

I admit that it was difficult to find writing inspiration for a topic after [KPop Demon Hunters](https://wooski.substack.com/p/kpop-demon-hunters). Prior to that, my interest in K-pop had plateaued as there weren’t new structural or thematic shifts to ponder about. After KPDH though, most discussions revolved around the film’s impact.

For my law firm’s holiday party in December, our team went to a concert of artist and client [Blood Orange](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jtwPHHmOSVs&list=RDjtwPHHmOSVs&start_radio=1). The artist’s skillful mastery of multiple instruments onstage inspired something inside me, and I was reminded of how obsessed I used to be with playing the piano, drums, and percussion before college. I installed Logic Pro again and started making hip-hop and EDM beats. I even checked out [Suno](https://www.billboard.com/video/sunos-ai-model-is-disrupting-music-business-mikey-shulman/) to see what the fuss was with AI music and was astonished by how easily it pumped out a melody.

These experiences in the past few months got me to tackle music from a different angle. I started approaching how the songs were structured, what kind of beats or leads comprise certain sections, and think about why the artists and producers made certain decisions.

Because ultimately, understanding the music business requires attachment to the music itself. For me at this moment, I’m finding new ways to enjoy and understand K-pop again. 2026 is feeling good.
